sublanguage: DCL (Data Control Language) — they control who is permitted to run operations on database objects, as opposed to DML, which changes the data itself
least-privilege: It follows least privilege — the app gets only the two rights it needs, so a successful injection on that account still can't DELETE, DROP, or alter the table
role-revoke: Both Jim and Pam immediately lose SELECT on employee, because they held it through the role — revoking from the role revokes for every member at once
sqlite-no-dcl: SQLite is a serverless, single-file database with no user accounts, so it has no GRANT/REVOKE at all — access control there is the OS file permissions; the exam's Oracle engine does support DCL
